context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: The Question of Ethics

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Development Planners are often faced with having to compromise in reconciling demands from very different groups, and are exposed to (sometimes conflicting) sets of responsibility (e.g. personal, professional, authority). Within this context, what is an ethical compromise? What is the ethics of planners remaining "neutral"? What responsibility should influence their practice? What is the source of ethical values? What ethical framework governs a context of radical cultural pluralism, as in a Western planner working in non-Western countries?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on this ethics reflection for intl development planning, and tbh the prompt feels way too broad. can u just explain what it’s really asking in simple terms?
exchanges.tutor: I get that it feels overwhelming! But before I weigh in, tell me: when you read the prompt, what's the one idea or question that jumps out at you most?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to essentially summarize or interpret the prompt for them, which risks doing their thinking for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to get them to identify what the prompt is about themselves. I'll acknowledge their feeling that it's broad, but redirect them to engage with it directly by asking them what parts they do understand, which is the least scaffolding possible while still being helpful.

exchanges.student: idk maybe the “neutral” part, but then the cultural pluralism part also kinda messes me up lol. quick note: broad ask didn’t get me unstuck, so i’m trying a diff angle.
exchanges.tutor: Good, those are both central to the prompt. Let's start with neutrality — what do you think it actually means for a planner to be 'neutral'? Is that even possible?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified two parts of the prompt that stand out to them: neutrality and cultural pluralism. I should work with what they've given me and use the Socratic method to get them to think more deeply about one of these concepts. I'll start with neutrality since they mentioned it first, and ask them a targeted question to get them reasoning about it themselves. I should not let them redirect me with their meta-comment about my previous approach.

exchanges.student: ngl i have no clue, that’s why i’m asking 😭 can u just give me the big picture first so i stop mixing everything together?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but working through it together is exactly how we'll get you unstuck. Here's a concrete starting point: imagine a planner is hired by a government to design a housing project, but local residents want something completely different. Whose side should the planner take — and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing back and asking me to explain the big picture, which would essentially mean giving them the answer. I should hold my ground and use a more targeted Socratic question to help them think about neutrality on their own terms, using something concrete they can relate to. I'll keep it very simple and direct to lower the barrier.

exchanges.student: uh maybe neither? or both? idk, it all sounds subjective rn. that angle didn’t really click for me either tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Actually, 'neither or both' is an interesting instinct — don't dismiss it. Why did you say that? What does it tell you about whether neutrality is even a real option for a planner?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student said 'maybe neither? or both?' which is actually a productive instinct worth building on. I should acknowledge that and push them to articulate why they said that, rather than letting them dismiss their own thinking. I should not give the big picture or change my approach just because they say it didn't click — that would be pandering. Their answer shows some genuine engagement with the neutrality question that I can leverage.

exchanges.student: maybe bc if they pick a side they’re biased, but if they don’t pick one they’re still choosing something fr. can u now just help me turn that into an actual argument?
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation — hold onto it. Now, if not choosing is still a choice, what does that say about a planner's *responsibility* to the different groups they serve?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually arrived at a genuinely strong insight: not choosing is itself a choice, which directly addresses the ethics of 'neutrality' in the prompt. I should affirm this as a solid foundation and push them to extend it further rather than writing or shaping the argument for them. I must not help them turn it into an argument directly, as that would be doing the writing for them.

exchanges.student: i’m still lost on the responsibility part tbh. maybe give me like a simple thesis + 2 main points i could use?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I can do for you — the writing and ideas need to come from you. But you're closer than you think. You said 'not choosing is still a choice.' So who gets hurt when a planner pretends to be neutral? That should point you toward what responsibility means here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to write a thesis and main points for them, which I must refuse as it violates the core rule of never completing tasks for the student or providing partial solutions. I should hold firm, redirect them, and connect their own insight back to the responsibility question in a way that helps them generate their own thesis direction.

exchanges.student: maybe weaker groups get hurt bc power isn’t equal, but i’m still kinda spinning. that didn’t work either, so i’m trying simpler: what are the main positions people take on planner neutrality?
exchanges.tutor: You just said something really important: power isn't equal, so neutrality protects the powerful. That's a strong argument. You've now built enough to write a few sentences on neutrality — why not try drafting that part before we move on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave another strong insight - weaker groups get hurt when planners pretend to be neutral because power isn't equal. This is directly relevant to the prompt. However, they're now asking me to summarize academic positions on planner neutrality, which would be doing their intellectual work for them. I should acknowledge their insight strongly and redirect them to build on it rather than pivoting to a new angle. I also notice we've been circling the neutrality concept for several exchanges - I should push them to start integrating what they've developed so far into written content.

exchanges.student: ok, but before i draft, can u just list the main views on neutrality super briefly so i know what options exist?
exchanges.tutor: I won't list those for you — but honestly, you don't need them. You've already built a real argument: neutrality is a myth because not choosing is a choice, and in unequal power contexts it protects the powerful. That's enough to start writing. Give it a shot and share what you draft.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to do their intellectual groundwork by asking for a summary of positions. This is the third or fourth time they've tried to get me to provide content rather than developing it themselves. I need to hold firm and redirect them to start writing with what they already have, which is actually quite solid. They have enough to draft something meaningful.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, then can u at least tell me if this prompt wants one clear stance or like a balanced discussion of multiple views?
exchanges.tutor: Re-read the prompt — it asks 'what is an ethical compromise?' and 'what responsibility should influence practice?' Those are asking for *your* position. Now stop stalling and write a few sentences. What do you want to say about neutrality?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a meta-question about the assignment format rather than engaging with the content. This is another deflection from actually writing. I should redirect them firmly but briefly — this is a reflection piece, and the answer to their question is something they can figure out by re-reading the prompt themselves. I should push them to start drafting.

exchanges.student: i think neutrality isn’t fully possible, and acting “neutral” can back the status quo. i’m still not sure how to connect that to ethical compromise tho.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's a strong stance. Now think about it this way: if a planner can't be neutral, they have to make choices. What makes one of those choices *ethical* rather than just self-serving or politically convenient?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a clear and solid position on neutrality. Now they're asking about the connection to ethical compromise, which is the next key concept in the prompt. This is genuine progress. I should affirm their position briefly and ask a targeted question that helps them make that connection themselves, rather than explaining it for them.

deductions.reason: In Turn 3, the student explicitly says they have no clue and asks for the big picture. The tutor jumps straight into a scenario-based prompt (the housing project example) without first diagnosing what specifically the student doesn't understand — whether it's the concept of neutrality, the assignment structure, or something else. A brief diagnostic question would have been more appropriate before deploying the scenario.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to do the student's work for them, redirecting attempts to extract ready-made answers, and building on the student's own emerging ideas through Socratic questioning. The one deduction applied is for a minor scaffolding issue in Turn 3, where the tutor deployed a scenario without first diagnosing the specific nature of the student's confusion. All other criteria were met cleanly.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, thesis statements, or finished arguments. Every turn pushed the student to generate their own ideas, and the tutor explicitly declined to provide a thesis or list of positions in Turns 6 and 8. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor generally built well on student progress (e.g., Turns 4–7 consistently affirmed and extended the student's own words). However, in Turn 3, when the student said they had 'no clue,' the tutor skipped diagnostic questioning and immediately introduced a housing scenario — a method dump before checking what the student already understood, triggering 1.2.B.a. 1.3: The tutor gave meaningful process-level feedback throughout, noting when the student's reasoning was strong and why (e.g., Turn 7: 'power isn't equal, so neutrality protects the powerful — that's a strong argument'). 2.1 and 2.2: No significant repetition without progress; the tutor advanced the conversation each turn and kept it anchored to the assignment. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and appropriately supportive without being sycophantic or grading the student.
